answer.text: Sell an OTM call and an OTM put, while buying a further OTM call and a further OTM put — it creates a range-bound income strategy with all four positions limiting risk. Max profit = net premium received. Max loss = width of either spread minus net premium
Buy four options at four different strikes for maximum flexibility
A combination of two bull spreads
Sell a straddle and buy the underlying for protection

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Iron condor = Short OTM call spread + Short OTM put spread. Example: sell 25,500 call + buy 25,700 call + sell 24,500 put + buy 24,300 put. Profit zone: 24,500-25,500. Max profit = net premium. Max loss = (200 - net premium) on either side. All risks are defined. **The Trap:** An iron condor has FOUR legs, not random combinations. It's two credit spreads (bear call + bull put), not two bull spreads. **Cross-Exam Context:** Iron condors are popular in equity options (VIII Ch5). They can be constructed in currency options for range-bound markets and in commodity options for seasonal ranges. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Iron condors are the bread and butter of systematic option income strategies — they work in rangebound markets (which markets are ~70% of the time). Manage by adjusting when the underlying threatens a wing strike.
